name: Lagrange Multipliers
tagline: Optimize a multivariate function subject to equality constraints by setting gradient(f) = sum of lambda_i * gradient(g_i). The lambdas measure the constraints' shadow prices.

blocks.text: To find local extrema of f(x_1, ..., x_n) subject to constraints g_1(x) = 0, ..., g_k(x) = 0, the necessary condition is that the gradient of f be a linear combination of the gradients of the constraints: gradient(f) + sum of lambda_i * gradient(g_i) = 0. The lambda_i are called Lagrange multipliers. Geometrically: at an extremum, you can't move 'along the constraint surface' without changing f.

The method gives n + k unknowns (n coordinates plus k multipliers) and n + k equations (n gradient conditions plus k constraint equations). Solving the system identifies candidate extremum points. You then evaluate f at each candidate to find the actual max or min.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: The constraint surface is like a fence. The function f has level curves. An extremum on the fence happens where a level curve is TANGENT to the fence -- the gradients align. Lagrange multipliers formalize this alignment.

whenToUse: Constrained optimization in 2 or more variables.
Distance from a point to a curve, surface, or higher-dimensional object.
Maximum/minimum of f along a path defined by g(x) = c.
Economics / portfolio problems where lambda is interpretable as a 'shadow price' of a constraint.
recipe: Identify the objective f and the constraints g_1, ..., g_k = 0.
Compute the gradients of f and each g_i.
Set up the system: gradient(f) + sum of lambda_i * gradient(g_i) = 0, plus the original constraints.
Solve the system. Often algebra reduces all variables to functions of a single lambda.
Evaluate f at each candidate and take the smallest (for min) or largest (for max).
commonPitfalls: Forgetting that gradient(f) is the gradient of the OBJECTIVE, not the Lagrangian. The sign convention for lambda is consistent but easy to mix up.
Solving only the gradient equations and skipping the constraint equations -- you must use both.
Failing to check the second-order condition -- Lagrange gives candidates, not classification.
Confusing equality constraints (Lagrange) with inequality constraints (KKT, a generalization).
Squaring the objective to avoid sqrt without realizing this preserves the optimum (true for non-negative objectives like distance squared).

appearsIn.title: Distance from Origin to a Plane
